section.title: The Scroll and the Lamb
section.sectionRationale: Chapters 4 and 5 are one continuous vision, but the scroll now supplies the throne room with its drama and its crisis. No one is found worthy to open it, John weeps, and then a Lion is announced who turns out to be a slaughtered Lamb. This is the christological hinge of Revelation's opening vision, the moment the whole book pivots from the settled throne to the Lamb who alone can unseal history, so it stands as its own unit before the seals begin to break in chapter 6.
section.thesis: No one in heaven, on earth, or under the earth is found worthy to open the scroll of God's purposes until the Lion of Judah appears, and He is a Lamb who was slain; His blood-bought worthiness turns John's weeping into an ever-widening chorus of worship that sweeps from four living creatures and elders to countless angels to every creature in creation.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the sealed scroll and the search for one worthy to open it; the Lion who is a Lamb, standing as though slain, taking the scroll; the new song, celebrating His ransomed and reigning people; and the song swelling outward into a universal doxology to the Lamb and the One on the throne.

units.label: The sealed scroll and the search for one worthy

verses.text: Then I saw a scroll in the right hand of the One seated on the throne. It had writing on both sides and was sealed with seven seals.
And I saw a mighty angel proclaiming in a loud voice, “Who is worthy to break the seals and open the scroll?”
But no one in heaven or on earth or under the earth was able to open the scroll or look inside it.
And I began to weep bitterly, because no one was found worthy to open the scroll or look inside it.

units.label: The Lion who is a Lamb

verses.text: Then one of the elders said to me, “Do not weep! Behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the {{rhiza|Root of David}}, has triumphed to open the scroll and its seven seals.”

Then I saw a {{arnion|Lamb}} who appeared to have been {{sphazo|slain}}, standing in the center of the throne, encircled by the four living creatures and the elders. The Lamb had seven horns and seven eyes, which represent the seven Spirits of God sent out into all the earth.
And He came and took the scroll from the right hand of the One seated on the throne.

When He had taken the scroll, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb. Each one had a harp, and they were holding golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints.

And they sang a new song: “{{axios|Worthy}} are You to take the scroll and open its seals, because You were slain, and by Your blood You {{agorazo|purchased}} for God those from every tribe and tongue and people and nation.
You have made them to be a kingdom and priests to serve our God, and they will reign upon the earth.”

units.label: The doxology of all creation
units.note: to Him and to the Lamb

verses.text: Then I looked, and I heard the voices of many angels and living creatures and elders encircling the throne, and their number was myriads of myriads and thousands of thousands.
In a loud voice they were saying: “Worthy is the Lamb, who was slain, to receive power and riches and wisdom and strength and honor and glory and blessing!”

And I heard every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying: “To Him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be praise and honor and glory and power forever and ever!”
And the four living creatures said, “Amen,” and the elders fell down and worshiped.

rhiza.gloss: Root. The Root of David draws on Isaiah 11:1 and 11:10, where a shoot rises from Jesse's stump and the nations rally to it. Christ is not only David's descendant, his branch, but also his root, the source of David's line and kingship, present before and greater than the king He descends from.

arnion.gloss: Little lamb, a diminutive form John uses twenty-eight times in Revelation as his favorite title for Christ. It carries the tenderness of a young, vulnerable lamb even while this Lamb bears seven horns of full divine power and receives the worship of all creation, holding together weakness and majesty in one figure.

sphazo.gloss: To slaughter, to slay violently, the verb used of sacrificial victims. The Lamb stands alive yet bears the marks of having been sphazo, slaughtered, a permanent reminder in heaven's throne room that His worthiness rests on a real, bloody death, not a symbol of one.

axios.gloss: Worthy, deserving. In chapter 4 the Creator was declared axios because He made all things. Here the Lamb is declared axios on different but equal grounds, because He was slain and by His blood purchased a people, so that creation and redemption together ground the praise of heaven.

agorazo.gloss: To buy in the marketplace, to purchase or ransom. The Lamb's blood is the purchase price for a people drawn from every tribe, tongue, people, and nation, language that recalls a slave market and casts redemption as a real, costly transaction that changes ownership from sin and the beast to God.

basic.context: John sees a scroll in God's right hand, sealed shut, and a search begins for someone worthy to open it. No one in all creation qualifies, and John weeps bitterly. Then an elder announces the Lion of Judah has triumphed, but when John looks he sees not a lion but a Lamb, bearing the marks of slaughter, standing at the very center of the throne. This Lamb takes the scroll, and heaven erupts in an ever widening song of worship that spreads from the throne's inner circle to countless angels to every creature everywhere.
basic.hermeneutics: The scroll pictures God's sealed plan for history, judgment and redemption bound together, and the crisis is that plan cannot go forward until someone worthy comes to enact it. People debate exactly what the scroll contains and why reigning 'upon the earth' in verse 10 is sometimes translated as a present reality and sometimes as a future hope, but the passage's clear center is unmistakable: Jesus is worthy, and He is worthy specifically because He was slain.
basic.originalLanguages: John's favorite name for Christ here is arnion, a little lamb, used again and again through Revelation, a word that holds together vulnerability and the seven horns of full power. The Lamb was sphazo, slaughtered, real sacrificial language, and by that blood He has agorazo, purchased, a people for God. The elders declare Him axios, worthy, on the ground of that costly purchase, the same word used of the Creator in chapter 4, now applied to the Redeemer.
basic.typology: The Lion of Judah fulfills Jacob's blessing over his son in Genesis 49, and the Root of David fulfills Isaiah's promise of a shoot from Jesse's line that the nations would seek. The Lamb who was slain fulfills every sacrificial lamb of Israel's worship, especially the Passover lamb whose blood spared God's people from judgment. And the purchased kingdom of priests fulfills God's promise at Sinai that Israel would be a kingdom of priests to Him.
basic.christ: Christ is announced as the conquering Lion but revealed as the slaughtered Lamb, holding together in one figure the triumph of a king and the humility of a sacrifice. He alone is worthy to unseal God's purposes for history because His victory was won at the cross. He receives the same worship given to the One on the throne, an early and unmistakable declaration of His full deity, and He is the one whose blood forms a redeemed people into a kingdom and priesthood.
basic.god: God holds history's scroll in His right hand, but He does not act alone; He has purposed from eternity that His Son, the slain Lamb, would be the one to unseal His plan. The Father who is worthy as Creator in chapter 4 is joined here by the Son who is equally worthy as Redeemer, and all creation is drawn into worshiping them together, a picture of the shared, undivided glory of God.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 49:9-10 gives the first promise of the Lion of Judah's scepter. Isaiah 11:1 and 11:10 promise the Root of David, a shoot the nations will seek. Exodus 19:6 first calls Israel a kingdom of priests, now fulfilled in the Lamb's purchased people. Revelation 1:5-6 already anticipated this, praising Christ who freed us by His blood and made us a kingdom and priests.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible has been building toward one question: who can be found worthy to carry out God's purposes for a broken world? Here the answer arrives, not a conquering general but a slaughtered Lamb, and the ancient promises of a Lion from Judah and a Root of David converge on Him. His blood purchases a people from every nation, and all creation joins its voice to declare Him worthy, the climax toward which every earlier promise of a rescuer was pointing.

context.literary: Chapters 4 and 5 form one unbroken heavenly vision. Chapter 4 fixed our eyes on the throne itself; chapter 5 introduces the scroll and its crisis, resolved by the appearance of the Lamb, so that the entire judgment sequence to come, seals, trumpets, and bowls, flows out of this single scene of the Lamb's worthiness being established and celebrated.
context.historical: Written to churches facing pressure to honor Caesar's claims to worldwide rule, this vision answers with a rival throne room where the true king is not a warlord but a slaughtered Lamb, and where worthiness is measured not by conquest but by sacrificial love, a message that reoriented how persecuted believers were to see power and victory.
context.canonical: This chapter gathers the Lion of Judah from Genesis 49, the Root of David from Isaiah 11, and the sacrificial lamb imagery running through the entire Old Testament sacrificial system, and fuses them into the fullest New Testament portrait of Christ as both triumphant King and slain Redeemer, a scene later echoed whenever Revelation returns to worship the Lamb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal that Jesus Christ alone, by virtue of His sacrificial death, is worthy to enact God's sovereign purposes for history's judgment and redemption, and to draw every reader into the same worship that fills heaven once this is seen.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes a heavenly reality and its effect on all creation; it is not prescribing a specific liturgy to copy verse by verse, though it has shaped Christian hymnody profoundly. The abiding truth is the worthiness of the slain Lamb and the response of worship that truth demands.

debates.question: Why is Christ announced as a conquering Lion but revealed as a slaughtered Lamb?

views.label: The Lamb reinterprets the Lion
views.case: John hears the language of military triumph, the Lion of Judah has 'triumphed,' but when he looks he sees a Lamb bearing the marks of slaughter, showing that Christ's conquest was accomplished precisely through His sacrificial death, not despite it; the cross is the victory, not merely a prelude to a separate future victory.

views.label: Both images hold together as complementary truths
views.case: Revelation elsewhere pictures Christ as a warrior king with a sword from His mouth and a name of Faithful and True; the Lion and Lamb titles are not in tension but describe one person from two true angles, His authority to judge and reign, and the sacrificial ground on which that authority rests.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage refuses to separate Christ's kingship from His cross; His right to rule and to unseal history's judgments is inseparable from His having been slain, so worship of His power and worship of His sacrifice are one and the same act.

debates.question: Does 'they will reign upon the earth' in verse 10 describe a present reality or a future hope?

views.label: A present, already unfolding reign
views.case: Some manuscripts and translations render this as a present tense, 'they reign,' fitting Revelation 1:6 where believers are already called a kingdom now, and Christ's own present session at God's right hand, so redeemed believers already share in Christ's authority in some measure before His return.

views.label: A future reign yet to be consummated
views.case: Other manuscripts and most translations render it as future, 'they will reign,' fitting the larger pattern in Revelation where the saints' full reign with Christ over the earth awaits His return and the new creation, described later in Revelation 20:6 and 22:5.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the purchased people of the Lamb are destined to reign with Him; the manuscript question over the verb tense affects only whether that reign is already begun in seed form or belongs entirely to the age to come.

study.originalLanguages: John's favored title for Jesus across Revelation is arnion, a little lamb, a diminutive that captures both the tenderness of a young lamb and, paradoxically, the seven horns of complete divine power He bears. This Lamb was sphazo, slaughtered, the same verb used of sacrificial victims whose blood was shed for atonement, and by that blood He has agorazo, purchased, ransomed a people out of the marketplace of sin and the beast's dominion. The elders declare Him axios, worthy, using the identical word applied to the Creator in chapter 4, now grounding worthiness in redemption rather than creation alone, while the title rhiza, Root of David, reaches back to Isaiah's promise that Messiah is not only David's heir but the very source of David's throne.

typology.fulfillment: Jacob's ancient blessing that the scepter would not depart from Judah is fulfilled in Christ's triumphant right to reign.
The shoot from Jesse's stump that the nations would seek is revealed as Christ, both David's descendant and his source.

typology.type: The sacrificial lamb of Israel's worship, especially the Passover lamb
typology.fulfillment: Every lamb slain under the old covenant finds its meaning in the Lamb once slain whose blood purchases and protects His people forever.

study.christ: Christ is introduced by title as the Lion of Judah and the Root of David, the fulfillment of Israel's royal hope, but when John looks he sees a Lamb standing as though slain, and it is this slaughtered Lamb, not a warrior on horseback, who proves worthy to take the scroll from God's hand. His worthiness rests entirely on His sacrificial death, by His blood He purchased people from every tribe, tongue, people, and nation, forming them into a kingdom and priesthood. He receives worship identical to the One on the throne, from the same living creatures and elders, then from countless angels, then from every creature in the universe, an unambiguous declaration that the Lamb shares the full worship and glory due to God alone.
study.god: God the Father holds the scroll of history's purposes in His right hand, but He has ordained that only His Son, proven worthy by sacrificial death, would enact those purposes. The chorus of chapter 4, which praised the Father as worthy Creator, is here joined by an equally thunderous chorus praising the Son as worthy Redeemer, and the two hymns merge into one final doxology addressed jointly 'to Him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb.' This is the throne room's decisive revelation that redemption is as central to God's glory as creation.

crossReferences.note: Jacob's blessing first names Judah's line as bearing the ruler's scepter, fulfilled in the Lion of Judah.
The shoot from Jesse and the Root that the nations will seek, fulfilled in Christ, the Root of David.
God first calls Israel a kingdom of priests, a promise the Lamb's blood now fulfills for a people from every nation.
Already praises Christ who freed us by His blood and made us a kingdom and priests, anticipating this chapter's song.

study.oneStory: Since Eden the Bible has searched for one who could set right what sin broke, and here that search reaches its answer in heaven's throne room. No one in all creation is worthy to unseal God's purposes until the Lion of Judah appears, and He turns out to be a Lamb who was slain. His blood purchases a people from every nation on earth, fulfilling the ancient promises to Judah, to David, and to Israel as a kingdom of priests, and drawing all creation into one unending song of worship to Him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb.

sermon.title: Lamb on the Throne
sermon.why: Begg preaches directly on Revelation 5:1-14, tracing the sealed scroll, the search for one worthy, and the Lamb's triumph through sacrifice, drawing out the sweeping scope of the redemption purchased for every tribe, tongue, and nation, which is the exact center of this section.

quiz.q: Why does John weep in the opening scene of chapter 5?

options.text: No one in heaven, on earth, or under the earth was found worthy to open the sealed scroll.
options.note: Verses 3-4 give this exact reason for his grief.

options.text: Because the Lamb had not yet been slain.
options.note: The Lamb's death has already occurred; John weeps because no one was found worthy to open the scroll, not because the sacrifice was still future.

options.text: Because the twenty-four elders refused to worship.
options.note: The elders are shown worshiping throughout; that is not the cause of John's weeping.

options.text: Because the throne room had gone silent forever.
options.note: The throne room is filled with sound and song throughout; John weeps over the scroll remaining unopened.

quiz.q: What does John see when he looks for the Lion of the tribe of Judah?

options.text: A Lamb standing as though slain, at the center of the throne, who takes the scroll.
options.note: Verses 6-7 describe exactly this reversal of expectation.

options.text: A literal lion standing beside the throne.
options.note: The lion language is titular; what John actually sees is a Lamb, not a literal lion.

options.text: An empty throne with no one able to approach it.
options.note: The Lamb does approach and does take the scroll from the One on the throne.

options.text: One of the twenty-four elders taking the scroll himself.
options.note: An elder announces the Lion's triumph, but it is the Lamb, not an elder, who takes the scroll.

quiz.q: On what basis is the Lamb declared worthy in the new song of verse 9?

options.text: Because He was slain and by His blood purchased people for God from every tribe, tongue, people, and nation.
options.note: Verse 9 states this exact ground for His worthiness.

options.text: Because He created all things.
options.note: That was the ground for the Father's worthiness in chapter 4; the Lamb's worthiness here rests on redemption, not creation.

options.text: Because He defeated the beast in open battle.
options.note: No such battle has yet occurred in the narrative; His worthiness rests on His sacrificial death.

options.text: Because the elders chose Him by vote.
options.note: His worthiness is declared as an already established fact grounded in His blood, not decided by a vote.

quiz.q: What happens to the scope of worship as the chapter unfolds?

options.text: It widens from the four living creatures and elders, to countless angels, to every creature in all creation.
options.note: Verses 8-13 trace exactly this widening circle of worship.

options.text: It remains limited to the twenty-four elders alone throughout the chapter.
options.note: The elders participate throughout, but the worship expands far beyond them to angels and all creation.

options.text: It fades away once the Lamb takes the scroll.
options.note: The opposite occurs; worship grows louder and wider after the Lamb takes the scroll.

options.text: It is restricted only to beings in heaven, excluding earth and sea.
options.note: Verse 13 explicitly includes every creature in heaven, on earth, under the earth, and in the sea.

quiz.q: What does the Lamb's purchase accomplish for those from every tribe and nation, according to verse 10?

options.text: It makes them a kingdom and priests to God, destined to reign.
options.note: Verse 10 states this exact outcome of the Lamb's redemptive purchase.

options.text: It merely rescues them from physical danger on earth.
options.note: The purchase includes far more than physical rescue; it constitutes them as a kingdom and priesthood before God.

options.text: It exempts them from ever facing tribulation.
options.note: Revelation elsewhere shows the redeemed enduring tribulation; the purchase secures their identity and destiny, not exemption from all hardship.

options.text: It has no lasting effect beyond the moment of the song.
options.note: The song describes a permanent, established reality: a purchased kingdom and priesthood destined to reign.

questions: John wept because no one was found worthy to open the scroll, until the Lamb appeared. Where in your life do you need to remember that Christ alone, not your own effort, is worthy to bring God's purposes to pass?

The Lion of Judah turned out to be a slaughtered Lamb; His triumph came through sacrifice, not despite it. Where are you tempted to measure victory by worldly strength rather than by costly, sacrificial love?
The Lamb's blood purchased people from every tribe, tongue, people, and nation. How does this shape the way you see and love people who are different from you?

Heaven's worship widened from the inner circle to countless angels to every creature in creation. What would it look like for your own worship of Christ to grow wider and louder rather than staying small and private?
The redeemed are made a kingdom and priests, destined to reign. How does knowing your ultimate destiny is to reign with Christ change how you face today's struggles and disappointments?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote allows 'the sevenfold Spirit' as an alternate rendering for 'the seven Spirits of God,' the same fullness-of-the-Spirit imagery used already in Revelation 1:4 and 4:5, here tied directly to the Lamb who sends the Spirit into all the earth.

The BSB footnote notes the Greek can be read 'they will reign over' or, following the Westcott-Hort text, the present tense 'they reign upon,' a manuscript question that affects whether the saints' reign is depicted as already begun or entirely future.
The Textus Receptus adds the phrase 'Him who lives for ever and ever' to the elders' closing worship in some manuscript traditions, though the shorter reading is well attested and used here.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the repo's makor-scripture tool for Revelation 5:1-14. No em or en dashes present in the source verses or used anywhere in this file.
